truth.papers.A13.title: Microstructure and tensile behavior of Fe-8Mn-6Al-0.2C low density steel
truth.papers.A13.paper_scope: Investigation of microstructure and tensile behavior of Fe-8Mn-6Al-0.2C low density steel, focusing on transformation characteristic, mechanical stability of retained austenite, work hardening behavior, and fracture mechanism, with emphasis on TRIP effect.

prediction.papers.A13.title: Microstructure and tensile behavior of Fe-8Mn-6Al-0.2C low density steel
prediction.papers.A13.paper_scope: Investigation of microstructure and tensile behavior of Fe-8Mn-6Al-0.2C low density steel under different solution treatment temperatures, focusing on the TRIP effect, austenite stability, three-stage work hardening behavior, and deformation mechanisms.

truth.alloys.alloy_fe8mn6al02c.alloys_notes: Nominal composition given as Fe-8Mn-6Al-0.2C; measured compositions from Table 1: 0.15C, 7.35Mn, 6.00Al (wt%).

prediction.alloys.alloy_fe8mn6al02c.alloys_notes: Actual measured composition (wt%): C 0.15, Mn 7.35, Al 6.00, P <0.005, S 0.01, Fe Bal. Measured density is about 6.95 g/cm3, approximately 11% lower than pure iron.

truth.processes.proc_h70.description: Hot forged, homogenized at 1200°C for 2 h, hot rolled to 3 mm thickness (6 passes, start 1150°C, finish 850°C), water quenched to 550°C, air cooled to room temperature, solution treated at 700°C for 1 h and water quenched.
truth.processes.proc_h80.description: Hot forged, homogenized at 1200°C for 2 h, hot rolled to 3 mm thickness (6 passes, start 1150°C, finish 850°C), water quenched to 550°C, air cooled to room temperature, solution treated at 800°C for 1 h and water quenched.
truth.processes.proc_h90.description: Hot forged, homogenized at 1200°C for 2 h, hot rolled to 3 mm thickness (6 passes, start 1150°C, finish 850°C), water quenched to 550°C, air cooled to room temperature, solution treated at 900°C for 1 h and water quenched.
truth.processes.proc_h100.description: Hot forged, homogenized at 1200°C for 2 h, hot rolled to 3 mm thickness (6 passes, start 1150°C, finish 850°C), water quenched to 550°C, air cooled to room temperature, solution treated at 1000°C for 1 h and water quenched.

prediction.processes.proc_h70.description: Vacuum induction melting, heating at 1150 °C for 2 h, hot forging, air cooling to room temperature, homogenization at 1200 °C for 2 h, hot rolling to 3 mm thickness via 6 passes (start 1150 °C, finish 850 °C), water quenching to 550 °C, air cooling to room temperature, solution treatment at 700 °C for 1 h, water quenching.
prediction.processes.proc_h80.description: Vacuum induction melting, heating at 1150 °C for 2 h, hot forging, air cooling to room temperature, homogenization at 1200 °C for 2 h, hot rolling to 3 mm thickness via 6 passes (start 1150 °C, finish 850 °C), water quenching to 550 °C, air cooling to room temperature, solution treatment at 800 °C for 1 h, water quenching.
prediction.processes.proc_h90.description: Vacuum induction melting, heating at 1150 °C for 2 h, hot forging, air cooling to room temperature, homogenization at 1200 °C for 2 h, hot rolling to 3 mm thickness via 6 passes (start 1150 °C, finish 850 °C), water quenching to 550 °C, air cooling to room temperature, solution treatment at 900 °C for 1 h, water quenching.
prediction.processes.proc_h90.processes_notes: Specimen designated as H90. Exhibits duplex ferrite-austenite microstructure with the best combination of tensile strength and ductility.
prediction.processes.proc_h100.description: Vacuum induction melting, heating at 1150 °C for 2 h, hot forging, air cooling to room temperature, homogenization at 1200 °C for 2 h, hot rolling to 3 mm thickness via 6 passes (start 1150 °C, finish 850 °C), water quenching to 550 °C, air cooling to room temperature, solution treatment at 1000 °C for 1 h, water quenching.

truth.processing_steps.sample_h70.processing_s: Forged to rods with section plane 35 mm × 70 mm × 100 mm; air cooled to room temperature

truth.structures.sample_h70.overall_structure: Microstructure consists primarily of martensite (M) and spheroidal M5C2 carbide, with about 12.2% retained austenite. M5C2 carbide distributed at δ-ferrite grain boundaries and among martensite laths. Original austenite largely decomposed.
truth.structures.sample_h70.microstructure_lis: at δ-ferrite grain boundaries and among martensite laths
truth.structures.sample_h80.overall_structure: M5C2 carbide disappeared; martensite has reverse-transformed to austenite. Large amount of strip α-ferrite (α-F) observed. Microstructure consists of α-ferrite, δ-ferrite, and austenite (~20.7%).
truth.structures.sample_h90.overall_structure: Duplex microstructure consisting of α-ferrite, δ-ferrite and austenite (~33.8%). α-ferrite morphology changed from strip to fine blocky shape. No martensite before deformation.
truth.structures.sample_h90.microstructure_lis: blocky and lath-like retained austenite; blocky at triple junctions, lath-like between ferrite laths or embedded in ferrite grains
high after deformation, particularly in ferrite and martensite
truth.structures.sample_h100.overall_structure: Microstructure consists of α-ferrite, δ-ferrite, martensite and austenite (~12.9%). Martensite appeared again due to higher solution temperature.

prediction.structures.sample_h70.overall_structure: Multiphase microstructure consisting of martensite, delta-ferrite, M5C2 carbide, and retained austenite
prediction.structures.sample_h70.microstructure_lis: widely distributed both at delta-ferrite grain boundaries and among martensite laths
prediction.structures.sample_h80.overall_structure: Duplex microstructure of delta-ferrite, alpha-ferrite, and austenite; M5C2 carbide disappeared
prediction.structures.sample_h90.overall_structure: Duplex microstructure consisting of alpha-ferrite, delta-ferrite, and austenite with enhanced uniformity
prediction.structures.sample_h90.microstructure_lis: equiaxed austenite grains; banded ferrite grains crushed and separated
prediction.structures.sample_h100.overall_structure: Multiphase microstructure of martensite, delta-ferrite, alpha-ferrite, and retained austenite

prediction.interfaces.sample_h70.phase_evolution: M5C2 carbide distributed at delta-ferrite grain boundaries and among martensite laths.
prediction.interfaces.sample_h90.defect_interaction: Martensite formed inside austenite grains acts as a barrier for dislocation glide and as a site for dislocation accumulation and source of partial dislocations.
prediction.interfaces.sample_h90.interface_notes[0]: Blocky austenite at ferrite triple junction boundaries experiences triaxial stress state and transforms first; lath-like austenite undergoes rotations firstly during deformation.
prediction.interfaces.sample_h90.phase_evolution: During tensile deformation, austenite volume fraction decreases while martensite volume fraction increases. Blocky austenite transforms first, lath-like austenite rotates first.

truth.properties.sample_h90.mechanical.tensile: Three-stage work hardening: stage 1 rapid decrease (ferrite deformation), stage 2 slight decrease due to TRIP effect (true strain ~0.03-0.15), stage 3 rapid decrease (deformation localization of ferrite/martensite). True stress increased abruptly at strain ~0.03.
Work hardening rate vs true strain plots shown in Fig. 2b; three stages visible.
Stage 2 linked to TRIP effect; WH rate increased with strain in 0.03-0.15, then decreased gently

prediction.properties.sample_h90.mechanical.tensile: True stress increased abruptly at true strain of ~0.03; no yield point phenomenon observed.
increased with strain in true strain range 0.03-0.15, then decreased gently after 0.15

truth.characterization_methods.sample_h70.purp: Measurement of austenite volume fraction and lattice parameter; C content in austenite
truth.characterization_methods.sample_h70.key_: Austenite fraction before deformation 12.2%, after 4.5%; transformation ratio 63.1%.
truth.characterization_methods.sample_h90.purp: Austenite volume fraction and C content
truth.characterization_methods.sample_h90.key_: Before 33.8%, after 3.4%; transformation ratio 89.9%. C content in austenite 0.391 mass%.
truth.characterization_methods.sample_h100.key: H100 shows martensite, α-ferrite, δ-ferrite, and austenite; Fig. 1d.
truth.characterization_methods.idx_6.purpose: Element distribution in austenite and ferrite; identification of M5C2
truth.characterization_methods.idx_6.key_findi: Mn and Al partitioning between phases; used to identify carbide and measure austenite composition for Ms calculation.
truth.characterization_methods.idx_10.key_find: Density of the steel is about 6.95 g/cm3, ~11% lower than pure iron.
truth.characterization_methods.idx_11.key_find: Austenite 275 ± 10 HV, δ-ferrite 314 ± 15 HV, α-ferrite 374 ± 10 HV, Martensite 438 ± 15 HV (Table 2).

prediction.characterization_methods.sample_h70.key_: Microstructure consists of martensite, delta-ferrite, M5C2 carbide, and 12.2% retained austenite. M5C2 carbide widely distributed at delta-ferrite grain boundaries and among martensite laths.
prediction.characterization_methods.sample_h80.key_: M5C2 carbide disappeared. Large amount of strip alpha-ferrite observed alongside delta-ferrite and austenite (20.7%).
prediction.characterization_methods.sample_h90.purp: Austenite volume fraction determination, lattice parameter measurement, and C content in austenite calculation
prediction.characterization_methods.sample_h90.key_: Direct comparison method using (200)α, (211)α for ferrite and (220)γ, (311)γ for austenite. Austenite volume fractions measured before and after tensile deformation for H70, H80, H90. C content in austenite calculated from lattice parameter using Eq. (1).
prediction.characterization_methods.sample_h100.key: Martensite appeared again with 12.9% austenite retained. Delta-ferrite and alpha-ferrite also present.

truth.unmapped_findings.idx_0.value: The volume fraction of small angle grain boundaries increased greatly during deformation, exceeding 51% at true strain 0.27 for H90 (from 26% at 0.03, 31% at 0.10, 45% at 0.20).
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_1.value: Schmid factor distribution: at true strain 0.03, >70% of retained austenite had Schmid factors 0.45-0.48; average decreased with strain; at 0.20, >60% had Schmid factor <0.40; at 0.27, volume fraction of austenite with Schmid factor <0.34 decreased to 5%.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_2.value: Fracture mode of H90 quasi-cleavage with features: flat 'class cleavage' facets, micropores, tear edges, secondary cracks, dimples, short curved river pattern, herringbone crack.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_3.value: Ms and Md30 temperatures calculated using empirical equations (2) and (3); C content in austenite measured by XRD using Eq. (1): αγ = 3.578 + 0.0330XC + 0.0056XAl + 0.00095XMn.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_4.value: In conclusion, the best combination of mechanical properties was achieved for the steel solution treated at 900°C for 1 h, attributed to TRIP effect and high dislocation density, with UTS of 836.4 MPa, YS of 473.6 MPa, and TE of 32% (note: text mentions 846 MPa in abstract, but 836.4 MPa in conclusion; discrepancy kept as in paper).

prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_0.value: The steel exhibited three-stage work hardening characteristics during tensile test: stage 1 with rapidly decreasing work hardening rate associated with ferrite deformation; stage 2 linked to TRIP effect where work hardening rate increased with strain in true strain range 0.03-0.15 and then decreased gently after 0.15 due to competition between increased stress and stress relaxation; stage 3 with quickly decreasing work hardening rate associated with deformation localization of ferrite or martensite.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_1.value: For H90 during tensile deformation, blocky austenite grains positioned at ferrite triple junction boundaries transformed into martensite first due to higher localized stress and triaxial stress state, while lath-like austenite grains located between ferrite laths or embedded in single ferrite grains with lower Schmid factor underwent rotations firstly.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_2.value: The volume fraction of small angle grain boundaries in H90 increased greatly during tensile deformation, with values of 26%, 31%, 45%, and over 51% observed at true strains of 0.03, 0.10, 0.20, and 0.27 respectively.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_3.value: Fig. 7 shows XRD profiles and corresponding austenite fractions before and after tensile deformation for H70, H80, and H90, with martensitic transformation ratios of 63.1%, 71.9%, and 89.9% respectively (austenite fractions after deformation: H70 4.5%, H80 5.8%, H90 3.4%).
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_4.value: The fracture morphology of H90 shows quasi-cleavage fracture features including flat cleavage facets, micropores, tear edges, secondary cracks and a few dimples; the ductile fracture area is mainly deduced from austenite deformation and the quasi-fracture area is correlated with ferrite deformation.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_5.value: Conclusion states UTS of 836.4 MPa for H90, while Table 3 lists 846.4 MPa; the abstract states 846 MPa. The Table 3 value of 846.4 MPa is used in the properties field.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_6.value: With increasing solution temperature from 700 °C to 1000 °C, yield strength decreases gradually (642.1 MPa to 398.3 MPa), which is explained by higher austenite volume fraction inducing easier plastic deformation and by asymmetric distribution of ferrite and coarsening of grains according to the Hall-Petch relation.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_7.value: Fig. 2 true stress-strain curves and work hardening rate-true strain curves for all four steels are presented in the original paper but detailed numerical curve values are not fully transcribed in the text; some approximate data points appear in figure transcription tables.
